data.title: Enterprise Products Partners: AI Upside, Strong Yield, 1.7X Coverage
data.description:
Enterprise Products Partners (NYSE:EPD) offers dividend investors a high degree of cash flow and return resilience, which makes the midstream enterprise a highly recession-resistant business. Enterprise Products Partners also succeeds in consistently growing its cash flow over time while maintaining excess distribution coverage... which will give the MLP room to grow its distribution. With demand for energy only set to grow in the years ahead, in part due to the growing energy needs of the Data Center market, Enterprise Products Partners has attractive long-term DCF and distribution growth prospects.

Previous rating

I rated units of Enterprise Product Partners a strong buy-in March — A Rock Solid MLP Growth Play — as the midstream company is one of the largest master limited partnerships in North America with a dependable record of growing its distributions. In the first quarter, Enterprise Products Partners continued to excel in terms of distribution coverage and raised its dividend by 3.9% year-over-year. EPD's units sold off in April after the U.S. announced new import tariffs, and although units have somewhat recovered, they can be bought at a cheaper price than in March.

Highly resilient DCF trajectory, strong distribution coverage

Enterprise Products Partners is one of the largest MLPs in the U.S. and owns a collection of highly critical energy assets including natural gas, crude oil, refined products, and NGL pipelines. The midstream company also owned complementary energy assets such as deep-water docks, for energy raw materials, fractionation facilities, and storage tanks.

Natural gas liquids are the single biggest business for Enterprise Products Partners, and the midstream enterprise generated more than half of its $10.0B in gross operating margins — in the last trailing twelve months period — from NGL transportation and storage services.

In the first quarter, EPD's gross operating margin totaled $2.4B, showing a slight decline of 2.4% year-over-year, mainly due to lower average sales margins, lower deficiency revenues, and a decrease in ethylene export volumes in the petrochemical segment.

Enterprise Products Partners has been able to consistently expand its distributable cash flow, however, through organic growth in its core business, pipelines, and acquisitions. Last year, Enterprise Products Partners acquired pipeline operator Piñon Midstream for close to $1.0B to strengthen its market position in the Delaware Basin in New Mexico and Texas. In the first quarter, Enterprise Products Partners generated $2.0B in distributable cash flow, showing 5% year-over-year growth.

A key growth driver for Enterprise Products Partners going forward could be the aggressive build-out of Data Center infrastructure around the world in order to accommodate progress in artificial intelligence development. Business consulting firm McKinsey estimates that close to $50B will be spent just on building Data Centers, a big portion of which are going to be built in the U.S.

Enterprise Products Partners itself has listed Data Center growth as a catalyst for energy production. In fact, AI and Data Centers are named as the biggest drivers of incremental energy demand until the end of the decade, which creates a long-term catalyst for EPD's distributable cash flow growth. The increasing demand profile also suggests that Enterprise Products Partners is going to continue to seek acquisitions to broaden its pipeline footprint, especially into regions that serve the Data Center industry.

Going forward, I would expect EPD to specifically acquire pipeline capacity that strengthens its footprint in the Northeast of the U.S. which is where many Data Centers are located. Further, Enterprise Products Partners

Stable long-term investment returns and excess distribution coverage

EPD has been able to grow its DCF, while maintaining stable excess distribution coverage, in the last twenty years, however. Increasing macroeconomic uncertainty including the financial crisis, the crude oil price correction of 2016 as well as the COVID-19 pandemic all did not hurt the midstream firm's DCF growth prospects for long...

The main reason for the stability of EPD's investment returns is that the company enters its fee-based delivery contracts with its producer clients, which means the midstream enterprise has very low earnings and distributable cash flow volatility. In Q1'25, about 80% of the firm's gross operating margin was related to fee-based arrangements, showing 2 PP growth compared to FY 2024.

At the same time, Enterprise Products Partners has demonstrated an ability to maintain stable investment returns over the course of a business cycle, including during periods of arguably increasing macroeconomic stress, such as during the COVID-19 pandemic. Over the course of the last twenty years, Enterprise Products Partners generated an average return on invested capital of ~12% annually, with very low volatility in the return pattern. Enterprise Products Partners' historical investment returns have fluctuated in a very narrow range of 10-13% which creates the perfect foundation, together with a high percentage of fee-based contracts, to support a strategy of high capital returns.

What supports the investment case for Enterprise Products Partners is that natural gas demand is expected to grow at least until the end of the decade (and likely beyond that) which creates attractive long-term DCF and distribution growth prospects for unitholders of EPD. According to Kinder Morgan (KMI), a key pipeline operator in North America, natural gas demand is set to rise, driven by growing domestic industrial and power demand. Growing energy needs strongly suggest that EPD will build or acquire more pipeline capacity in the years ahead, especially in markets that are connecting high-potential plays like the Permian Basin.

In the long term, Enterprise Products Partners has grown key financial metrics like revenue and EBITDA very consistently... which is the result of a high representation of fee contracts in its business. I would expect EPD to continue to grow key measures like EBITDA at mid single-digits in the years ahead and expect a similar growth rate for the distribution.

Low earnings volatility and distribution growth

Low earnings and investment return volatility obviously translate to strong distribution coverage and DCF predictability... all of which work to lower capital risks for investors and ensure that unit holders are receiving a steady stream of income.

In terms of distribution coverage, Enterprise Product Partners offers exceptional value for dividend investors. In Q1'25, EPD achieved 1.7X distribution coverage, which compares to a distribution coverage ratio of 1.8X in Q4'24 and 1.7X for the entire fiscal year.

Due to its high distribution coverage and, in part, acquisition-driven DCF growth, Enterprise Products Partners raised its distribution a total of 3.9% year-over-year to a current quarterly rate of $0.535 per unit.

EPD’s valuation

Units of Enterprise Products Partners have sold off drastically during the April ‘tariff crash’, but have since staged a solid rebound and are trading almost back at the pre-crisis valuation. Currently, units of Enterprise Products Partners are valued at an enterprise-value-to-EBITDA ratio of 9.7X, compared to a 3-year average EV-to-EBITDA ratio of 9.5X. Investors therefore pay a very small premium to the historical valuation of 2%.

Rival midstream companies like Kinder Morgan, Energy Transfer (ET), and Enbridge (ENB) are valued at enterprise-value-to-EBITDA ratios between 8.0X at the low-end (for Energy Transfer) and up to 12.3X for Enbridge. I generally like the midstream sector for its distributable cash flow visibility as well as low variability in returns... which makes pipeline operators perfect investments for retirees who have a need for highly predictable income certainty.

Considering that Enterprise Products Partners is expected to grow its EBITDA ~6% next year to $10.8B and that the distribution is set to grow in the years ahead, I believe units are still an attractive buy at the current valuation. Enterprise Products Partners is also likely going to grow its pipeline network through new targeted acquisitions, potentially in the high-potential Permian Basin, and with a high degree of excess distribution coverage present, I believe units could revalue to an 11-12X EV-to-EBITDA ratio... implying a fair value range of $35-39 per unit.

Risks with EPD

Enterprise Products Partners is growing its EBITDA and distributable cash flow and has through its growth projects a high degree of earnings and DCF visibility that I feel lowers risks for the midstream platform and its investors. What would change my mind about Enterprise Products Partners is if the midstream were to see a deterioration in its distribution coverage ratio (to less than 1.5X DCF) or if its distribution were to grow at a slower rate going forward.

Final thoughts

In my opinion, Enterprise Products Partners remains a no-brainer buy for dividend investors who value a high degree of distributable cash flow visibility and excess dividend coverage that gives the company leeway to increase its distribution going forward. Investors can reasonably expect a mid-single-digit growth rate in its distributable cash flow as well as distribution rate in 2025 and beyond, while the high degree of excess coverage should provide a limit on the MLP's downside. With shares trading for less than 10X EBITDA, I believe EPD remains a rock-solid choice for dividend investors and investors potentially get some capital appreciation potential here as well.

data.title: TSLT: Robotaxi Is A Big Catalyst For Tesla, But Maintain Sell On TSLT
data.description:
I last covered the T-REX 2X Long Tesla Daily Target ETF (BATS:TSLT) in early 2024 with a sell rating, as I was concerned about the TSLT ETF's decay from volatility.

For most of the past year, my recommendation had looked like a disaster, as Tesla's shares soared after President Trump was re-elected to the White House and "First Buddy" Elon Musk's fortunes looked bright. At one point in late 2024, the TSLT ETF had soared by over 300% since my article (Figure 1).

However, in recent days, the TSLT ETF has plummeted back to reality, as the Musk-Trump relationship soured, with Elon Musk seemingly calling for the impeachment of the President in one of his tweets on June 5th, 2025 (Figure 2).

As one can imagine, Tesla (TSLA) shares did not respond positively to the deterioration in relations between Elon Musk and Donald Trump, with TSLA shares plummeting by 14% on June 5th and TSLT shares plunging by 28% in sympathy.

TSLT's volatility over the past year encapsulates perfectly the risks and opportunities with leveraged ETFs. In my opinion, retail investors should stay away from these products.

At the end of the day, Elon's outburst may alienate his right-wing supporters, since many have sworn loyalty to Donald Trump above all else. Since Elon has already alienated progressives with his involvement in the Trump administration, losing conservative consumers could be a huge blow to Tesla's fortunes.

I continue to rate TSLT a sell.

Brief Fund Overview

The T-REX 2X Long Tesla Daily Target ETF is a leveraged single-stock ETF that aims to provide daily returns that are twice the return of Tesla, Inc. stock. The TSLT ETF achieves this levered return by holding cash plus total return swaps struck with large investment banks that are reset nightly to maintain 200% exposure to net assets (Figure 3).

As TSLA shares soared, the TSLT ETF had gotten quite popular with short-term speculators. At one point, the TSLT ETF had nearly $1 billion in assets under management ("AUM"), as investors loved the financial leverage of the product (Figure 4).

Leverage Works Both Ways

However, leverage works both ways, and as Tesla's vehicle sales slipped in recent months due to backlash against Elon Musk's perceived involvement in the Trump administration, TSLA shares have fallen by over 26% YTD, leading to a painful 62% plunge in TSLT shares (Figure 5).

Readers may wonder, why is the TSLT ETF down 62% when the underlying stock is only down 26% YTD? Isn't that 2.4x downside capture?

TSLT's problem is due to the 'daily reset' feature of the fund. Imagine if we had started with $100 invested in the TSLT ETF. If TSLA shares returned 5% on day 1, our investment in the TSLT ETF (ignoring friction costs) will grow to $110 (2 times 5% return). However, if TSLA shares lost 5% on day 2, our investment in the TSLT ETF will fall to $99.00, less than twice the 2-day compounded loss of 0.25% or $99.50. This effect is commonly called 'volatility decay'.

While volatility decay may seem small on a day-to-day basis, when we compound the effect over many days, volatility decay can cause leveraged ETFs like the TSLT ETF to significantly underperform their expected returns.

In fact, according to the manager's estimates, if volatility is high enough, a positive return in TSLA shares may actually turn into negative returns for the TSLT ETF. For example, at a volatility rate of 75%, a 30% one-year return in TSLA shares may become a 3.7% loss in the TSLT ETF (Figure 6).

This volatility decay 'feature' of the TSLT ETF is the primary reason why I assigned a sell rating to the TSLT ETF in my initiation article.

TSLT May Be Useful As A Swing Vehicle

However, it is not all bad news. As can be seen in TSLT's epic 2024 rally, if one has the foresight to buy the TSLT ETF at the lows, one can benefit from another feature of leveraged ETFs, namely, 'convexity'.

Going back to our numerical example above, assuming on day 2, TSLA shares gained 5% instead of losing 5%. Our investment in the TSLT ETF will grow to $121.00, more than twice the theoretical 2-day compounded return of 10.25% or $120.50. When streaky stocks like TSLA go on a multi-week run, a leveraged ETF like TSLT can produce significantly higher returns than 2x the underlying stock.

However, in my opinion, the only real 'winners' in leveraged ETFs are the issuers and market makers, who get to collect fat management fees and trading commissions as speculators buy and sell these products in a frenzy.

For most investors, I believe the best strategy on leveraged ETFs is to stay away.

Investors considering the TSLT ETF should make sure they read and understand these warnings from FINRA and the SEC.

Tesla's Vehicle Fortunes May Be Dimming

As I mentioned above, Tesla's sales performance has been poor in 2025. I believe one of the main reasons has been the company's stale product portfolio, which has been static for several years while competitors have been releasing exciting new models (Figure 7).

The most recent major vehicle release by Tesla was the Cybertruck in 2023, which has been a huge disappointment, in my opinion. Recall, when Elon Musk first teased the Cybertruck in 2019, Tesla boasted about thousands of pre-orders from fans of the EV-maker.

In fact, at launch, Tesla had a reported 1.9 million vehicle orderbook and 5-year wait times for the Cybertruck. Elon Musk predicted that Tesla would sell 250,000 Cybertrucks every year. However, almost 2 years since launch, the Cybertruck has sold less than 50,000 vehicles, amidst clunky styling and quality issues.

Elon Musk Alienating Everyone

Making matters worse is the fact that Elon's personal brand has become toxic to progressive consumers across the world, as he has pivoted to the right. This has directly led to lost sales for Tesla, as many progressives (the target demographic for Tesla's environmental EVs) have vowed never to buy Tesla.

However, with Elon Musk now going after the Republican champion, Donald Trump, Elon Musk risks alienating Republicans and right-winged consumers. Will Elon end up alienating both progressives and conservatives? Should such a polarizing figure remain the CEO of Tesla?

Robotaxi Launch In 1 Week

Of course, supporters of Tesla like Ark Invest's Cathie Wood have moved past stagnant vehicle sales in 2025 and have pinned their valuation models on Musk's grandiose vision of robotaxis and humanoid robots.

In fact, ARK attributes more than 60% of its projected $7 trillion in 2029 market capitalization estimate for Tesla to the robotaxi opportunity, never mind that Tesla's full self-driving ("FSD") software has yet to achieve the L4 autonomy that is required for robotaxi services. (Figure 8).

Therefore, Tesla's June 12th target launch date for its robotaxi service will be a key 'make or break' catalyst for the company. Expectations are high, and Musk's outburst against Donald Trump could be a sign that he is buckling under the stress of meeting the deadline.

Conclusion

TSLT's performance in the past year succinctly demonstrates the risks and allure of leveraged ETF investments. TSLT's shares soared in 2024, as rising optimism over Tesla's prospects coupled with positive convexity allowed the ETF to return over 300% at one point. However, negative leverage as Tesla's sales slowed and volatility decay has also caused the TSLT ETF to lose over 60% YTD.

Overall, I believe the only winners in the leveraged ETF game are the fund managers who can earn big management fees and the market makers who earn trading commissions.

Investors who share Elon Musk's vision for robotaxis and humanoid robots are encouraged to buy actual shares and avoid the negative volatility decay. However, I fear Elon Musk may be alienating consumers both left and right, and there may be nobody left to buy Tesla products.

I continue to rate TSLT a sell.

data.title: Badger Meter: Not Worth 50x Earnings As Growth Starts To Slow
data.description:
I follow the water industry and have covered quite a few names in that space on Seeking Alpha over the years. I like to invest in both water utilities and also the companies that provide infrastructure for the water sector more broadly.

Water is attractive in that it's hard to disrupt technologically. It should also enjoy increased investment over time. For one, there is the need to replace aging water systems. With the Flint water crisis, for example, we saw that systemic underinvestment in this vital infrastructure can lead to significant human consequences. There are also likely to be higher regulatory thresholds for water service going forward, particularly in areas such as the treatment of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), the so-called forever chemicals. Environmental concerns are also likely to lead to more investment in water reuse and conservation efforts.

I have made the case for owning water infrastructure and services companies such as Ecolab (ECL) in the past, even at relatively high valuation multiples. I am willing to look past the starting price to some degree for a high-quality water company. That said, there's a limit to how far that generosity runs.

Today's example, Badger Meter (NYSE:BMI) falls into the category where the valuation has become difficult to justify. With the most recent run-up in the share price, Badger Meter is now trading at close to 55 times trailing earnings and about 50 times forward earnings. The Badger Meter has been on my watch list for a long time, and it's something I would like to own at the right entry point.

That said, we aren't anywhere near a suitable entry point today. In fact, we're getting to the level where even long-time shareholders might consider selling their positions because it's hard to imagine this company maintaining this valuation multiple going forward.

It's not just earnings, either. Consider that this water company is now up to nearly 9x revenues. Just look at this chart of Badger Meter's price-to-sales ratio over the past 25 years:

A significant chunk of Badger Meter's historic returns have come from multiple expansions in addition to the growth in its underlying earnings per share.

The Bull Case: Tailwinds And Improved Business Quality

For those unfamiliar, Badger Meter offers tools for measuring and keeping track of water usage. In addition to smart meters and related equipment for water utilities, Badger Meter has several other ancillary services. It has an increasing array of equipment and software systems for leak detection, water quality monitoring, and consumer engagement tools for monitoring and managing water usage.

The company had often grown revenues in the mid-single digits range historically, which is not too surprising given that it primarily sold water equipment, normally a fairly stable market.

However, growth greatly accelerated starting in 2021:

Specifically, while Badger Meter has averaged a 9% compounded growth rate over the past decade, this has surged to a 15% average growth rate over the past five years.

This has been driven in significant part due to its software offerings, which allow for more recurring revenue growth rather than the top line being driven by one-time hardware sales.

In addition, U.S. water utilities are in a heavy CAPEX cycle right now, in large part due to aging infrastructure. Some recent data suggests that about 19% of installed water mains are beyond their useful lives, with the average age of a failing water main being 53 years old. There are an estimated 260,000 pipe failures per year. After decades of underinvestment, there could be a large addressable market in trying to close this gap.

This tailwind has helped funnel more money toward Badger Meter, especially as it relates to water usage analytics and optimization expenditures. I believe some of that faster growth is likely to be sustainable. There's plenty of room left in the market for conversion to smart meters and more modernized water infrastructure. Future stakeholder demands around conservation and efficient water usage will only intensify. Put simply, industry tailwinds are strong, and Badger Meter isn't likely to return to low-to-mid single-digits revenue growth in the near future.

That said, I don't think the recent 15% annualized revenue growth rate is sustainable, either. Higher interest rates are likely to reduce capital expenditures on water systems, at least at the margins. I don't expect a huge tailwind from federal government spending on water projects under the current administration. And a softer economy, in general, could curtail willingness to spend at quite the same rate that we've just witnessed.

We are already seeing indications of this in the company's outlook. The company's Q1 results were strong, with revenues growing at 13.2% year-over-year and earnings per share topping expectations.

However, the company offered this more measured comment about its forward outlook:

"As previously communicated, we will face difficult prior-year comparisons in the upcoming second quarter of 2025. Nevertheless, the attractive fundamentals of the water industry, and our ability to add value to customers with an innovative and reliable portfolio of solutions, support our average high-single-digit long-term revenue growth outlook."

Straight from the horse's mouth, we see that Q2 is not likely to be particularly strong, at least not compared to Badger Meter's recent standard.

Additionally, management stated that its long-term revenue growth outlook is in the high single digits.

So, we know that Q2 is likely to be quite soft and a significant deceleration from the past year. In addition, investors should be planning on something closer to 8-9% long-term revenue growth, rather than the 15% annualized rate that Badger Meter has posted over the past five years. Despite the clear indication of a slowdown ahead, Badger Meter stock shot up to new all-time highs following that Q1 earnings report:

BMI Stock Verdict

Badger Meter is trading at or near record high valuations on metrics such as P/S, P/E, and EV/EBITDA.

It appears that investors are paying this lofty price due to Badger Meter's exemplary operating results over the past few years. That makes sense, to an extent.

However, the company has already noted that Q2 results are likely to be significantly softer and that the longer-term revenue growth rate will dip back into the high single digits.

I understand that Badger Meter has become a significantly better business over time. Recurring revenues tied to smart meters and related software products deserve a higher multiple than one-time revenues tied to individual meter sales. But there is some limit to how high the multiple can go. And I think we're near that point now that Badger Meter's revenue growth is about to decelerate.

I could see a case for paying 30-35x earnings for Badger Meter. The company is in the right place at the right time. There's a lot of structural demand for improved water infrastructure. And investors are willing to pay high multiples for predictable and consistent growth. Management has also done an excellent job operationally in terms of seizing the market opportunity and leaning into the demand for software solutions.

But let's be realistic here. BMI stock has already rallied more than 40% from the April lows. Shares are trading at 50x forward earnings and 8.5x revenues. This is a hard valuation to defend for a company whose own management has suggested that revenues will grow in the high single digits over the longer term.

data.title: XLU: A Look At The Structure And Composition Of This Popular Utility ETF
data.description:
The Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLU) is one of the largest and most popular exchange-traded funds tracking the domestic utilities sector. We can see this by looking at its assets under management and comparing the figure to some of its peers. Here is a chart that does so:

Russell 1000 Utilities RIC 22.5/45 Capped Index

$1.52 billion

Fidelity MSCI Utilities Index ETF (FUTY)

MSCI USA IMI Utilities Index

As we can see, the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF is by far the largest index exchange-traded fund that focuses on the domestic utilities sector. Indeed, the second-largest exchange-traded index fund that tracks this sector (the Vanguard Utilities Index Fund ETF Shares) is less than half its size.

While most exchange-traded funds are highly liquid due to their open-ended structure, the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF is especially so. It has an average daily volume of 11.87 million shares over the past three months. At a share price of $80.86, that means that approximately $959.808 million worth of shares change hands every day. As we can see from the chart above, this is a significant proportion of the fund’s total assets under management. As such, investors in the fund should not have any difficulty getting into or out of it at a moment’s notice. Indeed, an investor should typically be able to get into or out of this fund without having their trade have any real impact on the fund’s share price. Traders or other investors who value liquidity might value this aspect of the fund’s profile.

With that said, the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF tracks an index of utility companies. Utilities are generally favored by more conservative investors who wish to have a degree of safety with their investments. Utility companies in general are very slow-growing entities that are unlikely to deliver the capital gains that we frequently see in other sectors of the economy. We can see this quite clearly by looking at the share price of XLU compared to the S&P 500 Index (SP500) over the past ten years:

As is clearly shown, the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF underperformed the S&P 500 Index on a price performance basis over the ten-year period that is shown. This is not atypical, as many, if not most, utilities do not grow their earnings at as high a rate as companies in some other sectors. It is rare to see a utility deliver more than 7% to 8% earnings per share growth annually, and some of them are lower than that. Thus, we would ordinarily not expect them to deliver the same level of capital gains as a technology company that is growing its earnings per share at a 10% or greater rate.

In order to provide investors with a competitive total return, utility companies tend to pay a significant proportion of their profits out to their investors in the form of dividends. The dividends that they pay represent an investment return that is in excess of any capital appreciation that their stock price delivers. When we combine this with the fact that many utilities have low valuations relative to other sectors (due to the slower earnings per share growth), it results in utility companies having fairly high yields compared to the rest of the market. We can actually see this by looking at the yields of the largest holdings of XLU. Here are the largest positions in the fund:

These are many of the largest electric and natural gas utility companies in the United States, which is pretty much what investors in this fund should expect. Here are the yields of these companies:

The Wall Street Journal provides the following yields for the major domestic broad-market indices:

As we can see, the largest holdings of XLU have significantly higher yields than any of the domestic broad-market indices, with the notable exceptions of Constellation Energy and Vistra Corporation. This will ordinarily be the case due largely to the relatively low growth rates of utility companies and the fact that they pay out a fairly large proportion of their earnings to investors.

The high yields of many utility companies are important to investors who purchase shares of the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF, and it also gives it one of its more attractive characteristics. The fund collects all of the dividend payments that it receives from the utilities in its portfolio, subtracts its own expenses from the amount, and then distributes the rest to the fund’s investors. This gives the exchange-traded index fund itself a higher yield than most broad-market index funds. As of the time of writing, the fund yields 2.76%, but this yield will naturally fluctuate somewhat over time. However, it should still, in many cases, have a higher yield than a fund tracking the S&P 500 Index or a similar broad-market index.

Characteristics of the Utilities Select Sector SPDR

As noted in the introduction, the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF is designed to track the performance of the Utilities Select Sector Index. S&P Global provides this description of the Utilities Select Sector Index on its website:

All components of the S&P 500 are assigned to one of the eleven Select Sector Indices, which seek to track major economic segments and are highly liquid benchmarks. Stock classifications are based on the Global Industry Classification Standard. Capping is applied to ensure diversification among companies within each index.

This really does not tell us very much about the index. S&P Global is the creator of the Utilities Select Sector Index, so we might expect its website to provide a bit more information than this. Fortunately, it does if we look at the index’s fact sheet, which can be downloaded in PDF format from the website. The fact sheet includes this helpful graphic showing some of the most important things for investors to know:

Basically, what S&P Global did is that it put each company that is included in the S&P 500 Index into one of eleven categories based on its primary business. These eleven categories are:

  • Communication Services (telecommunications, media & entertainment, video gaming, and companies that distribute content through proprietary platforms),
  • Consumer Discretionary (automobiles, household durable goods, leisure products, clothing, and hospitality companies),
  • Consumer Staples (food manufacturers and distributors, beverages, tobacco, and manufacturers of non-durable household goods),
  • Energy (anything involving the production, transportation, and refining of hydrocarbon products, as well as companies that provide services to those firms),
  • Financials (banks, financial services companies, brokerages, consumer finance companies, and insurance companies),
  • Health Care (health care providers, manufacturers of medical equipment, pharmaceuticals, biotechnology companies, and medical research firms),
  • Industrials (defense contractors, building product manufacturers, electrical equipment manufacturers, machinery manufacturers, construction companies, as well as companies that provide transportation services),
  • Information Technology (manufacturers of communications equipment, cellular telephones, and computers, along with semiconductor manufacturers),
  • Materials (chemical firms, wood and timber producers, glass producers, steel producers, paper products companies, and anything involving mining),
  • Real Estate (real estate developers, property managers, and equity real estate investment trusts),
  • Utilities (electric distributors, natural gas distributors, water distributors, independent power producers, and energy traders).

Once a company is assigned to a category, it is included in one of the Select Sector Indices. In the case of the Utilities Select Sector Index that is tracked by the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF, it includes any company that is in the S&P 500 Index and classified as a utility using the above definition (this definition is also available on S&P Global’s website). The companies in this index are then weighted based on their market capitalization as of the rebalancing date (the second Friday of March, June, September, and December).

As we just saw, in order to be included in the Utilities Select Sector Index, a company must also be included in the S&P 500 Index. As such, this index only includes large-cap utilities, and it will not have the same breadth as some other indices that track utilities. The Utilities Select Sector Index only has 31 components, which is fewer than the 43 components in the Russell 1000 Utilities RIC 22.5/45 Capped Index.

The basic strategy of XLU is to simply purchase the constituents of the Utilities Select Sector Index in whatever proportion they are represented in the index. So if, for example, a given company were to be 5% of the index, then this fund would put 5% of its assets into that particular company’s common stock. The fund changes its portfolio whenever the index changes. This naturally results in the fund’s portfolio being very close to the index itself:

We can see that the fund’s portfolio has slightly different weightings to some companies than does the index, but otherwise it is pretty close. This is partly because the fund holds a small amount of cash and cash equivalents that represent dividends that have not been paid out. As the cash and cash equivalents are actually part of the fund’s portfolio, they reduce the weightings compared to the index (the index does not include any cash or cash equivalents). Apart from this, though, we can see that the fund is simply purchasing the securities in the index in roughly the same proportions as they actually are in the index.

This means that the XLU will have fewer holdings than an index fund that tracks a broader market-capitalization index. In particular, this fund has limited to no exposure to small-capitalization or mid-capitalization companies. That might be important for potential investors to consider before purchasing this fund. Some of the fund’s peers (shown in the introduction) track indices with more constituents, including smaller companies, and so they will have more holdings and may be able to provide more diversification. How important this is depends on an investor’s exact goals and what they are trying to achieve with their portfolio.

XLU Specific Factors

The Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF is one of the oldest exchange-traded index funds tracking the utility sector, which might account for some of its popularity. This fund has an inception date of December 16, 1998, so it has been around for quite some time. Many of the peer funds shown earlier have later inception dates. We can see that here:

Fund Name

Inception Date

The Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF

December 16, 1998

Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Utilities ETF (RSPU)

November 1, 2006

Vanguard Utilities Index Fund ETF Shares (VPU)

January 26, 2004

iShares U.S. Utilities ETF (IDU)

June 12, 2000

Fidelity MSCI Utilities Index ETF (FUTY)

October 21, 2013

As expected, and as clearly shown, the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF is the oldest of the passively managed utility funds. While older funds are not necessarily “better” per se, they do tend to have a first-mover advantage. Many of the State Street SPDR funds, a family that includes the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF, are the first ones that investors think of when they want to make a sector-specific investment and as we will see later in this article, the peer funds shown above do not boast significantly lower fees or have any other characteristic that gives them a real marketing advantage. The biggest difference between these funds is the index that they track.

Reasons To Own Utilities

Utilities have long been a favorite of risk-averse investors for a few very good reasons. One of the most important of these is that these companies are highly resistant to recessions or economic shocks. Utilities, in general, provide products that are critical for modern life. After all, there are very few people who do not have electric services in their homes or businesses. There are very few people in the United States who do not have running water in their homes and businesses. Finally, very few buildings do not have some method of space heating installed (which may be an electrical, natural gas, propane, or oil system). In fact, in many areas, habitability laws require that any occupied structures have some or all of these things. In most cases, these things are provided by utilities, and people have come to depend on them being available on demand. Thus, nearly everybody will prioritize paying their utility bills over making discretionary expenditures during periods of time in which money gets tight. This allows utilities to be highly resistant to recessions, since people will cut back on just about anything else before they stop paying utility bills. This characteristic allows most utilities to enjoy relatively stable revenues, cash flow, and profits no matter what the conditions in the broader economy are like.

As such, XLU might be appropriate for anyone who is seeking a relatively safe asset that can be a core long-term holding in their portfolio. As most utilities are relatively resistant to recessions, the fund should decline less than funds invested in companies that are more exposed to discretionary consumer spending. Businesses also cut back on capital spending during recessions, so companies that are highly exposed to business spending are also likely to be more adversely impacted by such an event than the components of the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF. The fact that this fund’s portfolio consists entirely of large-capitalization stocks adds somewhat to the safety factor here, as large-capitalization companies are even more likely to remain stable than smaller ones with less geographic reach.

This means that XLU could be a reasonable purchase when it is expected that a recession will soon affect the United States. It could also work well for someone who simply wants to have an asset that they do not need to worry too much about.

With that said, utilities do tend to have lower total returns than companies in other sectors. As we already saw, the price appreciation that XLU delivered over the ten-year period that ended on June 5, 2025, was less than half of what the S&P 500 Index delivered. Even if we adjust for the beneficial effects of the dividends paid out by the constituent companies, utilities were still worse than the S&P 500 Index (SP500TR):

Thus, there is a price to pay for safety in the form of lower total returns. The Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF works very well as a safe haven asset, but investors who want to maximize their total returns or grow their wealth will still want to ensure that they have exposure to other assets that have greater growth potential. In other words, sector diversification across a portfolio is key here.

Similar ETFs And Comparison

As we saw in the introduction, there are some other exchange-traded index funds tracking the utility sector. They have some similarities, primarily that their portfolios will include many of the same companies with the same fundamentals, but all of them track different indices and that means that their performance will be somewhat different.

One area in which the different utilities-focused exchange-traded index funds differ is in yield. We can see that here:

Fund Name

Distribution Yield

The Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF

2.76%

Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Utilities ETF

2.37%

Vanguard Utilities Index Fund ETF Shares

2.84%

iShares U.S. Utilities ETF

2.18%

Fidelity MSCI Utilities Index ETF

2.70%

One reason for this is that each of the exchange-traded index funds tracks a different index. These indices differ in their methodologies, and as such, they will have different weightings to different common stocks. This can contribute to yield differences simply because a fund that has higher exposure to higher-yielding common stocks will itself have a higher yield, all else being equal.

Of course, not all else is going to be perfectly equal, as some of these exchange-traded index funds have higher expenses than others. This is summarized in this table:

Fund Name

Expense Ratio

The Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF

0.08%

Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Utilities ETF

0.40%

Vanguard Utilities Index Fund ETF Shares

0.09%

iShares U.S. Utilities ETF

0.39%

Fidelity MSCI Utilities Index ETF

0.084%

As we can see, the XLU has the lowest expense ratio out of any member of this peer group. There are two other funds with expense ratios that are very close to it, though (the Vanguard Utilities Index Fund ETF Shares and the Fidelity MSCI Utilities Index ETF). This is important because expenses create a drag on the fund’s performance. In particular, the fund itself has to pay its expenses out of its investment returns. This reduces the fund’s performance compared to the index. The lower the fund’s expense ratio, the less it will underperform its index, all else being equal.

The underperformance of a fund relative to its index is referred to as the fund’s “tracking error.” We can see this tracking error when we compare the fund’s performance to that of the index. This information is summarized here:

The first row shows the performance of the fund’s portfolio, whereas the third row shows the performance of the index. As we can see, this fund does fairly well at delivering a performance that is pretty close to that of the index. Over a ten-year period, the fund only underperformed the Utilities Select Sector Index by fourteen basis points, which is not bad at all.

We also notice, however, that there was no period during which the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund outperformed its benchmark index. There are two reasons for this. The first is that the fund’s expenses create a permanent drag on its performance relative to the index. The index, after all, does not have an expense ratio. The second reason why this fund will underperform the index is that it keeps a small amount of its portfolio in cash at any given time. During most periods, cash and cash equivalents underperform the common stock of utility companies. Thus, the cash equivalents that the fund holds will slightly reduce its performance compared to the index, which is 100% common stock. This is a common problem with any index fund, and indeed, almost none of them will outperform their benchmark indices on a sustained basis.

All five of the funds above have delivered pretty similar performances over the ten-year period that ended on June 5, 2025. We can see that here:

As we can see, the price performance of all of these funds has varied from 89.45% to 99.35% over a ten-year period. Given this range, we can immediately see that all of them are going to be within a few dozen basis points of each other over any given twelve-month period. Thus, on a price-performance basis, none of them really has a huge advantage over the others.

This is also largely true once the distributions of the fund are included in the performance and we look at total return:

The Vanguard, iShares, and Fidelity funds were almost identical over the ten-year period. However, we can see that the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF and the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Utilities ETF both did a bit better. The Invesco fund is tracking an equal-weight index, which is a little different from the market-capitalization-weighted index tracked by XLU. In an equal-weighted index, every single constituent asset has the same weighting. Thus, that fund has a higher weighting assigned to smaller companies than the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF. In a situation where smaller capitalization companies outperform their large-cap brethren, that fund will have an advantage. This was the case over the ten-year period that is shown, but it will not always be the case. As such, the Invesco fund may not be directly comparable from a performance perspective. The XLU was the best performer on a total return basis out of the remaining funds, which might actually be due to its smaller number of constituents, as it had less exposure to the weakest companies or utility start-up firms that never survived. The lower expense ratio of the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund could also play a role here.

In any case, we can see that historically, XLU has not had a performance disadvantage when compared to its peers.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF is one of the oldest and most well-known utility-focused index exchange-traded funds on the market. XLU is by far the largest exchange-traded fund tracking the domestic utility sector, and it is frequently considered to be a solid benchmark to see how other utility funds are doing. This fund has a relatively low tracking error, low expenses, and a respectable yield. It should work pretty well for any investor who is seeking exposure to the utilities sector but does not want to pick and choose between individual companies or actively managed funds.

This article answers these three main questions about XLU:

  1. Is XLU appropriate for risk-averse investors?
  2. Does XLU display recession resistant qualities?
  3. Would XLU be considered a defensive ETF?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for education purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: EFT: Improved Valuation, But This Floater Fund Is Still A Sell
data.description:
The Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust (EFT) is a closed-end fund that has become a fairly popular method of earning income over the past few years. This is at least partly due to the fund’s fairly solid performance in the face of rising interest rates. Over the past five years, shares of the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust are up 4.86%. This compares to a horrible 16.69% decline of the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG), which tracks domestic investment-grade bonds:

Another possible reason for this fund’s recent popularity is that it boasts a remarkably high distribution yield, and it has been able to increase it over the period. Five years ago, in June of 2020, the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust was paying a monthly distribution of $0.0560 per share. Today, that figure has increased to $0.0940 per share, and it was even higher than that a few months ago:

As we can see, back in early 2024, the fund was paying a distribution of $0.1180 per share, which was more than double the amount that the fund was paying five years ago. This makes the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust one of the few debt-focused funds that actually benefited from the increase in interest rates that occurred over the period in question. This is due to the fact that this fund primarily invests its assets in floating-rate loans, which deliver higher coupon payments whenever short-term interest rates increase. The Federal Reserve’s interest rate hikes over the 2022 to 2023 period thus benefited the assets held by this fund, as opposed to hurting them.

Unfortunately, though, this works both ways, and floating-rate securities are hurt whenever the Federal Reserve reduces the target federal funds rate. This is the reason why the fund’s distributions have fallen since June of last year. The 100 basis points of rate cuts that were implemented over the September 2024 to December 2024 period reduced this fund’s income, and that reduced the amount of money that it has available to pay out to shareholders. Unlike traditional bonds, floating-rate securities do not go up in prices whenever interest rates decline, so this fund cannot use capital gains to offset the loss of income in an environment that is characterized by monetary easing. This could, unfortunately, weigh on the fund over the remainder of this year and, as such, investors who are still holding shares of the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust might want to consider selling it in favor of something that will benefit from falling interest rates.

Distribution Comparison

Despite its recent distribution cuts, the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust still has a fairly attractive yield today. As of the time of writing, this fund yields 9.20%, which is a lot higher than any of the major domestic fixed-income indices. We can see that here:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index

3.84%

Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index (JNK)

6.64%

Bloomberg US Floating Rate Note < 5 Yrs. Index (FLOT)

5.35%

As of the time of writing, domestic junk bonds still have the highest yields of any major fixed-income category. However, even these securities pale in comparison to what the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust offers. From the perspective of an income investor, the difference here is quite wide. A $1 million investment in the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust would produce $92,000 in annual income right now compared to only $66,400 annually from the junk bond index. An extra $25,600 per year in income can certainly buy quite a lot, and one of the reasons why we try to amass wealth is to purchase more things. This fund certainly appears to be more helpful than any of the major fixed-income indices in that respect.

However, it is worth noting that the current yield of the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust is not particularly high when compared to its peers. We can see that quite clearly here:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust

Fixed Income – Taxable-Senior Loans

9.20%

Ares Dynamic Credit Allocation Fund (ARDC)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Senior Loans

9.56%

BlackRock Floating Rate Income Trust (BGT)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Senior Loans

11.73%

Carlyle Credit Income Fund (CCIF)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Senior Loans

19.35%

First Trust Senior Floating Rate Income Fund II (FCT)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Senior Loans

11.87%

Invesco Senior Income Trust (VVR)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Senior Loans

12.19%

Nuveen Floating Rate Income Fund (JFR)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Senior Loans

12.27%

Pioneer Floating Rate Fund (PHD)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Senior Loans

9.22%

abrdn Income Credit Strategies Fund (ACP)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Senior Loans

15.92%

As we can immediately see, the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust has the lowest yield of its peer group. However, there is another fund shown (the Pioneer Floating Rate Fund) that has a yield that is relatively close to that of the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust. This is something that might prompt investors to favor a peer fund over the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust. After all, most investors who are interested in a floating-rate debt fund in the first place are looking to receive a significant amount of income from the assets that they already possess. As we have already discussed, higher-yielding assets are far more helpful in this goal than lower-yielding assets.

However, there is one thing that potential investors might want to consider here before discounting the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust entirely. As I showed in a recent article, there are a number of floating-rate funds that have not cut their distributions despite the fact that their incomes have declined significantly over the past year. The fact that they continue to pay out the same amounts that they did when the federal funds rate was 100 basis points higher has been destroying their net asset values and, by extension, their long-term sustainability. The funds that have cut their distributions over the past year – the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust chief among them – are the ones that also have the lowest yields (the abrdn Income Strategies Fund is the exception here as it has both a very high yield and it has cut the distribution in the past twelve months). Thus, the lower-yielding funds could prove to be the most sustainable and durable ones over the long term. Investors who are looking for something that they can hold for an extended period of time might wish to keep this in mind before eschewing the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust for a higher-yielding peer.

Recent Performance Update

We previously discussed the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust on November 24, 2024. There have certainly been quite a few events that have occurred since that time that have had effects on various asset types in the markets. However, most of these events have not had a significant impact on the price or overall performance of the floating-rate securities that the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust invests in. This is because there are very few things that move the price of these securities. A look at the iShares Floating Rate Bond ETF, which is an unleveraged vehicle that tracks the price of an index of floating-rate securities, shows that the only thing that occurred over the past ten years that moved their price more than a percentage point or two was the mass market selloff that occurred at the outbreak of the COVID-19 pandemic:

The biggest reason for this is that these securities deliver a yield that is based on the Secured Overnight Financing Rate plus some margin. They also reset their yield fairly frequently based on this benchmark, so they will pretty much always deliver a yield that is in line with the market rate of interest for a security with a given default risk. As such, the price will almost always be very narrowly range-bound right around their face values.

Without a doubt, the biggest thing that occurred between the date of the previous article’s publication and today that had a lasting impact on floating-rate securities was the Federal Reserve’s interest rate cut at the December 2024 meeting of the Federal Open Market Committee. This was the third such rate cut in 2024, and as we can see here, it brought the Secured Overnight Financing Rate down to under 4.40%:

While there were one or two instances where the Secured Overnight Financing Rate spiked above 4.4% following that meeting (in late December 2024, late March 2025, and late April 2025), it has generally been pretty consistently in a range from 4.30% to 4.40%. That is consistent with the Federal Reserve’s federal funds target range of 4.25% to 4.50%. While the Secured Overnight Financing Rate and the federal funds rate are not exactly the same thing, they tend to be very close together. Thus, we can see that the Federal Reserve’s December rate cut had the effect of bringing down the Secured Overnight Financing Rate.

Now, take a look at a selection of the fund’s security holdings that come from its third-quarter 2025 holdings report:

We can immediately see that every single one of these securities has an interest rate that is equal to the three-month Secured Overnight Financing Rate + XXXX%. This basically means that the security’s yield is equal to whatever the average Secured Overnight Financing Rate was over the preceding three-month period plus the stated percentage. As the Secured Overnight Financing Rate went down in December, and all of these securities are using the three-month trailing average (which would be roughly early March through today), this calculation would result in the security having a lower yield today than it did back in November 2024, when we previously discussed the fund. This means that the fund’s net investment income went down, all else being equal.

There was no other event that occurred during the past seven months since our last discussion on this fund that had as significant of an impact on the assets that it holds. Even the market sell-off that followed the imposition of the “Liberation Day tariffs” only caused the floating-rate securities index to decline by 1% to 2%, and it quickly recovered over the following days. We can see that in the ten-year price chart that is shown above. As such, there is little else that we need to consider when trying to understand the recent performance of this fund.

As the price of the assets held by the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust has been relatively stable since our last discussion, we might expect the share price of this fund to have been relatively stable over the period. However, that has unfortunately not been the case. We can see this here:

As we can see, the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust has seen its share price decline by 8.16% over the period. This is in direct contrast to both fixed-rate investment-grade bonds and the floating-rate securities index, as both assets have been relatively stable over the same period. This is also in direct contrast to the value of the fund’s portfolio. As we can see here, the fund’s net asset value only declined by 2.73% over the period in question:

The net asset value is the current market price of all of the fund’s assets minus its outstanding debts. As we can clearly see, the portfolio itself performed a lot better than the fund’s share price did.

One possible explanation for the difference in performance between the fund’s share price and the actual portfolio underlying the common shares could be that the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust cut its distribution at the end of 2024. Closed-end funds frequently see their share price decline whenever they cut their distributions, regardless of the reasons for the cut or the performance of the actual underlying assets. This is why we sometimes see very high-yielding funds priced at a premium even when they cannot cover their distributions.

In any case, the fact that the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust’s share price performed much worse than its net asset value over the period means that it should be a lot cheaper now than it used to be. As you may recall, the price of this fund was one of my complaints about it the last time that we discussed it, so the apparent price improvement here could be a welcome development.

The Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust did manage to do much better on a total return basis:

As shown, investors in this fund suffered a 3.88% total loss since our last discussion on the fund. This is far worse than what either investment-grade bonds or the floating-rate securities index delivered over the period, as both of those indices managed to deliver positive total returns. However, a 3.88% total loss is still a lot better than what is implied by the share price performance alone, and it occurred because the large distribution paid out by this fund offset some of the share price decline. However, a loss is still a loss, and investors who took my previous advice and sold this fund in November managed to avoid it.

About The Fund

The website for the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust states that the fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a very high level of current income. This is a reasonable objective given that the fund’s name suggests that it invests in various types of floating-rate debt securities. The fund’s website makes the same statement:

Provides broad exposure to the floating-rate loan market, providing diversified exposure to the asset class. Provides exposure to the loan markets’ many sectors, credit tiers and issuers.

As we saw in the introduction, the domestic floating-rate securities index has been pretty much flat over the past decade, with the exception of a very brief selloff at the start of the COVID-19 pandemic. However, this does not mean that it did not provide any investment returns over the period. We can see this by comparing the index’s price return with its total return:

As clearly shown, the price level of the index has been almost perfectly flat over the period. However, it still managed to provide investors with a 28.96% total return over the period. This is simply because the securities in the index were making interest payments to their owners the entire time. These interest payments serve as a source of income, so all of the total returns that investors in floating-rate securities received over the past decade were current income. As the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust invests in floating-rate securities, it would also have all of its total returns coming in the form of current income. Thus, the objective is quite reasonable.

The fund’s third-quarter 2025 holdings report shows that it is invested in a few different types of floating-rate securities, as well as invested in a few assets that are not floating-rate securities at all. Here is the asset allocation provided by this document as of February 28, 2025:

Asset Type

% of Net Assets

Asset-Backed Securities

6.1%

Common Stocks

2.1%

Corporate Bonds

5.8%

Preferred Stocks

0.1%

Senior Floating-Rate Loans

141.6%

Warrants

0.0%

Money Market Fund

2.2%

The asset-backed securities and the senior floating-rate loans are floating-rate securities that generally conform to the principles that we discussed earlier in this article. Common stocks, corporate bonds, preferred stocks, and warrants, however, are not floating-rate securities. I must admit that it is somewhat surprising to see fixed-rate securities (the corporate bonds and preferred stocks) in a fund such as this one, as these securities have vastly different characteristics from floating-rate assets. In particular, fixed-rate assets rise in price whenever interest rates decline, so they actually benefit from falling interest rates. That is the exact opposite of what we see with floating-rate securities that are hurt by falling interest rates. However, with that said, the presence of the fixed-rate securities will help soften the blow to this fund’s finances should interest rates decline going forward. It is unlikely that the beneficial impact on the fund’s portfolio from the rising prices of the corporate bonds and preferred stocks in a falling interest-rate environment will have a significant impact, though, simply because these securities only account for a relatively small proportion of the fund’s total assets.

The third-quarter 2025 holdings report states that the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust had $19,969,381 invested in corporate bonds and $368,302 invested in preferred stocks as of February 28, 2025. This gives the fund a total of $20,337,683 invested in fixed-rate fixed-income securities. This compares to total assets of $548,019,569 as of the same date. Thus, the fixed-income securities are only 3.71% of the fund’s assets as of the date of the most recent report. That is certainly not enough to have a significant impact on the fund’s portfolio, even if the price of them does rise somewhat.

With that said, the Federal Reserve’s current monetary easing stance has not been having the effect of reducing bond yields. In a recent article, I showed that yields on long-dated securities have actually been rising year-to-date despite the Federal Reserve holding interest rates steady following a four-month period of interest rate cuts in late 2024. This phenomenon is even more pronounced when we compare U.S. Treasury yields today to where they stood back on September 16, 2024, right before the Federal Reserve embarked on its monetary easing policy. Take a look at this comparison chart:

U.S. Treasury Term

Yield on September 16, 2024

Yield on June 6, 2025

Ten-Year

3.63%

4.51%

Twenty-Year

4.01%

4.99%

Thirty-Year

3.94%

4.97%

The federal funds target range dropped by 100 basis points over the period shown. Yet, we see that bond yields increased by 88 to 103 basis points depending on the term of the bond. This is a clear sign that the Federal Reserve’s interest rate cuts are not having the desired effect on longer-dated securities, the yields of which are set by the market and not by the dictates of monetary authorities. This could mean that the fixed-rate securities held by the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust might not rise in price even if the Federal Reserve proceeds with its plan to cut interest rates later this year. If that proves to be the case, then even the small hedge that this fund has against falling interest rates will fail.

As I showed in a previous article, the Federal Reserve remains committed to its plan to cut interest rates later this year. Today’s reasonable jobs report did slightly reduce the market’s expectations of a 25-basis-point cut in September, but that remains the most likely scenario with the market assigning a 51.2% probability that the federal funds rate will be 25 basis points lower at that time than it is today:

The market is then expecting a second 25-basis-point cut in December 2025, followed by two 25-basis-point cuts in 2026. We can therefore see that the future does not look particularly good for the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust. If the Federal Reserve actually follows through on the market’s (and the central bank’s own) expectations, then the fund’s income can be expected to decline over the next eighteen months. This fund does not have any significant exposure to anything that will deliver capital gains to offset the loss of income. It therefore seems likely that the distribution cuts will continue, and that seems likely to weigh on the share price going forward.

Distribution Analysis

As was shown in the introduction, the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust cut its distribution back in February of 2025. However, it still pays a monthly distribution of $0.0940 per share ($1.128 per share annually). This gives it a 9.20% yield at the current share price.

Let us have a look at the fund’s most recent financial statements to see how well it is doing, and strengthen our understanding of why the fund will likely be forced to cut its distributions if the Federal Reserve proceeds with the planned interest rate cuts.

The most recent financial report for the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust is the semi-annual report for the six-month period that ended on November 30, 2024. Obviously, this is a newer report than anything that we had available to us at the time of publication of my previous article on this fund.

For the six-month period that ended on November 30, 2024, the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust reported a net investment income of $16,922,705. This was not sufficient to cover the $17,725,651 that the fund paid out in distributions, but it got pretty close. As we can see here, the fund also got fairly close to covering its distributions solely with net investment income for the twelve-month period that ended on May 31, 2024:

As we can see, over the trailing eighteen-month period, the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust came very close to covering its distributions solely with net investment income. Only $1,730,878 had to come from a source other than net investment income. Due to the limited ability of floating-rate funds to generate capital gains due to the stability of their assets, we generally want a floating-rate fund to simply pay out its net investment income. That is what this one is doing, so everything looks fine here.

This does, however, reinforce my conclusion that this fund will be cutting the distribution once the Federal Reserve reduces interest rates further. After all, that will reduce the fund’s net investment income.

Valuation

Shares of the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust are currently trading at a 4.36% discount on net asset value. This is reasonably in line with the 4.22% discount that the fund’s shares have had on average over the past month.

It is also worth noting that this price is a lot better than the 1.21% premium on net asset value that the fund’s shares had the last time that we discussed it. It is generally a bad idea to purchase any fund at a premium, and it is certainly a bad idea to purchase a fund that is certain to cut its distribution in the near future at a premium valuation. It is not unexpected that the fund no longer has its premium, given that the shares have underperformed the fund’s portfolio over the past several months.

The current price is fairly reasonable given the long-term history of this fund. Over the past three years, the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust has traded at prices ranging from a 13.54% discount on net asset value to a 2.89% premium on net asset value. The average price over the period was a 5.06% discount, which is only slightly better than the fund’s price today.

Here is how the current price of the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust compares to its peers:

Expenses

The Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust has an expense ratio of 5.26% right now:
