Conclusion

In conclusion, the Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust looks better than it did back in November of 2024. However, this is only because the fund cut its distribution, and its valuation improved. The fundamentals for it are still generally negative, as the Federal Reserve is still planning interest rate cuts that will force it to cut its distribution further. This fund’s share price usually declines whenever it cuts the distribution, so investors will likely have further pain.

I am maintaining my sell rating on this fund as the expected policies of the Federal Reserve mean that investors would likely be far better off in other assets.

data.title: T-Mobile US: Growth Surpasses Expectations, Valuation Limits Upside
data.description:
T-Mobile US: Growth Outpaces, Valuation Holds Back

We initiate on T-Mobile US, Inc. (TMUS) with a Hold rating and $245 PT. T-Mobile offers mobile communications and connectivity services in the United States, Puerto Rico, and the US Virgin Islands, serving over 108mn customers through both branded and wholesale channels. Our thesis is based on a strong multi-year revenue outlook: We model T-Mobile generating $88.0bn in FY25E and $93.7bn in FY26E, running ahead of The Street consensus on stronger-than-expected ARPA gains, the rapid T-Fiber ramp and incremental T-Satellite launch.

Here's the conundrum: while management's execution on premiumization and cross-selling fiber/satellite products adds 1-1.5% pts to ARPA and $1-1.5bn equipment upside that's largely ignored by The Street, the market already ascribes this future growth at today's valuation. We apply an EV/Sales multiple of 4.15x on FY26E - premia justified by underappreciated FCF leverage from higher-margin segments, but partially offset by our cautious step-down as growth rates normalize. At our PT, we rate R/R as neutral, with capex intensity and tariff headwinds as risks to further multiple expansion. TMUS is executing on all growth levers, but at $245/share, we see its superior outlook reflected in today's valuation.

High-margin Fiber And Satellite Expansion: Measured Upside Drivers For Incremental ARPA And Cash Flow

The tech story remains mixed: Acceleration into high-margin fiber & satellite broadband represents a marked evolution of T's mix, but value creation will be a function of integration discipline and customer ramp. At least visibility has improved with the official close of the Lumos transaction, which sets T up to announce the T Fiber launch later this quarter (management explicitly frames this as the first step to "profitably servicing even more broadband customers" while leveraging the "go-to-market strengths" of its on-the-ground retail channel and 1MM+ fixed wireless waitlist). Notably, the T Fiber launch is also positioned to directly capture latent demand, particularly in markets where T can overlay its brand and retail throughput on top of a fiber footprint that historically lacked such scale.

Meanwhile, T's 100s of thousands of active beta users for T Satellite point to meaningful progress ahead of a July commercial launch (now with locked-in $10/mo pricing, and bundled for free in premium plan packages), a differentiated offering supported by the 550+ satellites already in orbit. All told, the math says these add incremental ARPA and new high-margin equipment & service revenue streams, but management's own guidance for the Lumos retail business calls for "slightly accretive to service revenues and neutral to adjusted EBITDA and adjusted FCF," appropriately tempering expectations as the integration plays out. For capital markets, the mix of upside options and measured execution presents a nuanced R/R: Fiber & satellite are well-positioned to unlock high-margin growth, but near-term valuation likely reflects much of the initial lift, requiring ongoing focus on customer ramp, cost discipline, and accretive rollout.

Persistent Capital Intensity And Tariff Headwinds: The Road To FCF Conversion & Multiple Expansion Remains Constrained, In Our View

For investors debating who has the longer growth runway, the capital intensity/tariff headwinds equation deserves a close look, as it adds a subtle but important constraint on T-Mobile's path to translating operational momentum into outsized incremental shareholder value. The margin arithmetic is not complicated: even as topline growth outlooks remain stellar, continued deployment of next-generation infrastructure (particularly fiber/satellite) implies elevated absolute and relative capital intensity as substantial upfront and maintenance capex is required to support growth initiatives.

Against this backdrop, the risk of tariff escalations on imported network equipment/devices (or even certain consumer hardware items) presents a clear headwind to operating margins, with management specifically citing limited ability to pass these along in a competitive environment. The business sense here is clear: each incremental dollar allocated to capex or absorbed in higher COGS is either margin lost or opportunity cost incurred relative to peers if they can negotiate, substitute, or pass along pricing more effectively.

While disciplined capital allocation & scale advantages may mitigate some of the impact, any persistent increase in capital intensity/tariff risk reduces the likelihood of meaningful multiple expansion, particularly as investors focus more on long-term FCF yield and margin improvement as justifications for premium valuation. All told, we see these headwinds as net-negative for the further upside from here, albeit partially offsettable, and supporting our cautious stance.

T-Mobile US: Above The Street On Revenue Growth

We model 8.1% FY25E revenue ahead of The Street 6.2% ($88.0bn vs. The Street $86.4bn) and 6.5% FY26E ($93.7bn vs. The Street $90.8bn +3.2%). Our thesis is underappreciated revenue/ARPA uplift from accelerated ramp of T-Fiber and introduction of T-Satellite, both of which lever T-Mo's 130mn mobile base and significantly outpace consensus' linear ramp.

Source: FMP FY25 FY26
Estimate # Analysts Estimate # Analysts
Revenue $86.42B 16 $90.79B 18
EBITDA $30.06B 16 $31.58B 18
Net Income $12.55B 16 $15.11B 18
EPS $10.70 15 $12.71 17
SG&A Expenses $22.95B 16 $24.11B 18

Q1 FY25 postpaid ARPA growth was +3.5%, the strongest Q1 in 8 years, supported by record fiber waitlist conversion and new premium rate plans, with fiber and satellite cross-sell expected to contribute an additional 1-1.5ppt to ARPA in late FY25. We also anticipate $1-1.5bn of incremental equipment revenue tied to device activations, and service revenue uplift from T-Satellite ($10/mo. beta launch not in most models). These drivers are not included in The Street forecasts, giving us confidence in our higher growth outlook; however, we highlight that cap intensity and potential tariff headwinds could still limit valuation upside, a detail the market seems to overlook.

Valuation

Our $245 PT on TMUS is based on a FY26E EV/Sales multiple of 4.15x applied to our above-consensus revenue forecast of $93.7bn. This represents our conviction in an extending momentum cycle from ARPA tailwinds, accelerated fiber rollouts, and T-Satellite monetization upside, which, we believe, is underappreciated by The Street.

Our target multiple is a deliberate step-down from 4.40x in FY25E, but continues to provide a premium relative to TMUS's historical median (3.4x, 96th pctile) and the peer median (2.5x, 94th pctile). We believe a premium is justified given T-Mo's greater visibility on multi-year topline and cash flow growth via cross-selling new technologies into its broad customer base. The market also materially underestimates the FCF leverage in T-Fiber and T-Satellite, given the high-margin nature of both will eclipse legacy mobile economics in the years ahead.

However, our HOLD rating reflects that the stock price already discounts the lift from both premium ARPA cohorts and new connectivity adjacencies, with multiple expansion limited by capital intensity and tariff headwinds. In summary, while we believe that TMUS's growth profile and business mix transformation is best-in-class across peers, it is appropriately (albeit amply) reflected in the current valuation.

Risks to Our Hold Rating

While our Hold rating and $245 PT on TMUS reflects our view of balanced R/R, and assumes execution on fiber, Sat, and premiumization, we see limited downside to our thesis from two main sources:

1) Elevated cap intensity and volatility on regulatory/tariff fronts could erode both NT FCF and our premium multiple; risk of further U.S. tariffs or inflationary shocks could compress margins, drive price increases, or slow network expansion, limiting FCF leverage that is at the core of our thesis. Any capex upside, or adverse regulatory actions (e.g., spectrum consolidation scrutiny, pushback after a large merger) would pressure TMUS's ability to attain the ARPA/service revenue growth we forecast.

2) We see ongoing, high-profile security incidents as a material risk: despite ongoing investment, multiple breaches of customer data (2021-24) led to large settlements, regulatory fines, and new FCC security requirements, raising the potential for further reputational hits, costly compliance burdens, or future litigation if gaps are not fully remediated. Any recurrences of operational/tech-control failures (delayed reporting, API misconfigurations, insufficient network segmentation) should be closely watched as it could materially affect both customer trust and cost structure.

Our base case assumes successful mitigation, but investors should watch cap intensity vs. guidance, regulatory developments (especially on data stewardship and network expansion), and the timing of material breach disclosures as key signals for potential downside to our multiple framework.

Conclusion

We retain our Hold rating on T-Mobile, as we believe shares fully reflect its industry-leading growth profile, driven by superior ARPA trends and outsized T-Fiber and T-Satellite cross-sell potential. While management's execution and future FCF leverage differentiate TMUS, we see near-term upside as capped given elevated capex intensity and valuation reflecting these premium drivers. Investors should remain selective at these levels, looking to capital allocation and regulatory/regulatory shifts as the most clear near-term catalyst for either new opportunities or re-pricing of risk.

data.title: AI Is A Threat To Most Stocks - But A Potential Catalyst For The REIT Sector
data.description:
I believe that artificial intelligence is the biggest innovation of our century, much bigger than even the Internet.

So far, all previous innovations were limited by human intelligence, but AI completely removes those limits. AI is far smarter and faster than humans in most cases and can be applied to almost anything.

It's already starting to transform the economy and reshape industries, but we are yet to see its full effect.

Today, most investors seem to think that AI will lift all boats by reducing costs and boosting profits.

But I think that it's the opposite.

I expect most businesses to suffer greatly from AI and see their profits and share prices decline in value as a result of it.

Amid the chaos, there's a surprising winner that I expect to become more valuable than ever before as a result of it, and that's real estate investment trusts (VNQ).

Yes, I know this may seem far-fetched. After all, what could be less "tech" than real estate? But that's the whole point here. Stick with me, and I will explain how REITs are set up to become one of the biggest beneficiaries of sentiment around AI and other sectors and create a lot of wealth for early investors over the coming decade.

The AI Revolution Will Hurt Most Businesses

Before discussing REITs, I need to explain why I believe that AI will hurt most businesses.

It seems counterintuitive to many. If businesses become more efficient, shouldn't they enjoy rising margins and profits? And yes, this is true in some cases, especially over the short run.

But when everyone gets efficient, no one gets a lasting edge.

Instead, I expect AI to break barriers to entry, leading to much more competition, lower pricing power, and declining margins.

Take my investment research business as an example. Before AI, producing high-quality research required some level of expertise and the ability to write a lot of content. The act of writing was in itself a barrier, as most people are not capable of writing so many articles. These barriers gave serious writers an edge.

But now, AI can generate hundreds of articles in just a few clicks. Those articles may not be as good, but they're good enough. It has unleashed a wave of new content online, saturating most niches. People's attention is finite, but the amount of content is now far bigger, making it a lot harder to ever get discovered.

The barriers have been broken, leading to far more competition, lower pricing power, and ultimately, lower profits.

Before AI, you would have had to work very hard to launch such a business, but you can now do it over a weekend with the help of AI.

The same dynamic is now starting to play out across most other industries.

You can create almost any type of business far easier than ever before and have near-perfect marketing, customer service, branding, etc., from day 1. With AI agents, it's as if you had the entire freelancer base of Fiverr working for you, nearly for free.

It democratizes capabilities that were once exclusive and breaks traditional moats like scale, teams, brands, and advertising. Ultimately, it will lead to far more competition and the pie will be shared among more businesses, resulting in lower profits for each individual company.

Businesses become commoditized and their value collapses.

REITs: The Ultimate Anti-AI Asset

Real estate is the exception because you cannot replicate a skyscraper in Manhattan, a warehouse near a strategic port, or a hospital in a growing sunbelt market.

In a world in which most businesses get disrupted, real estate will stand out as the "anti-AI" asset class to preserve and grow wealth.

The world will suddenly see a lot more value in scarce physical assets that remain essential to our society and have a lasting moat in their location (the land) and structure (construction cost).

In other words, real estate could regain its reputation as the ultimate store of value following the AI revolution, and this should lead to growing allocations toward real estate, leading to much higher prices.

Think of it like this: If investors are concerned about their stock portfolios (SPY) because most businesses are facing growing competition, eroding barriers to entry, and lower margins, they will need to reallocate into safer assets, and there's nothing better than real estate and essential infrastructure.

But there's more.

AI is expected to lead to mass deflation. The marginal cost of producing almost any goods or service is expected to go down significantly over the coming decade. Some AI experts like Elon Musk believe that this will bring an "age of abundance" with most goods and services costing much less than today.

We expect this deflation to then lead to much lower interest rates and possibly even a return to near-0% rate policies. This could then lead to significant cap rate compression and higher property values than ever before.

So all in all, the impact of AI could be extremely positive for real estate and likely push its prices to new all-time highs. We will still need to have a roof over our head, warehouses to store our goods, grocery stores to buy food, etc., and those will become the ultimate store of value in this new world we are going into.

The Set-Up for REITs to Win Big

If you want to win big in investing, you need to find a big trend and invest in it ahead of the crowd.

In the late 90s, many investors who predicted the rise of Internet companies made a fortune. In the early 2010s, the same was true for investors who saw the rise of cloud computing.

Today, I think that the biggest overlooked trend is the coming disruption to businesses caused by AI and the “flight to safety" in scarce real assets that it will create.

I think that this is the biggest of all trends, and yet, most of the market hasn’t recognized it.

So far, the market only seems to have realized the first-degree impact of AI, which is that chip manufacturers like Nvidia (NVDA) would benefit from it. There are also a lot of people who still think that “AI is overhyped,” as old habits die hard.

Therefore, it seems that 99.9% of the market has missed the narrative that AI will actually hurt most businesses, but end up benefiting real estate values as it leads to lower interest rates, and it regains its “store of value” reputation.

This is a huge opportunity for REIT investors in particular, because REITs are toady priced at decade-low valuations with many individual REITs trading at 30, 40, or even 50% discounts relative to the value of the real estate they own:

They have gone through a three-year-long bear market and are incredibly cheap due to fears of surging interest rates.

But as the narrative shifts to what I just described in this article, I expect an epic recovery in the coming years, pushing REITs to new all-time highs, and the upside potential is very significant.

Take the example of Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR).

The once popular industrial REIT has seen its share price crash by 56% since 2022, even as it has grown its cash flow by 25% since then. What this essentially means is that it is now 80% cheaper than it was in early 2022 - a real crash!

As a result, it's now trading at just 14x FFO and an estimated 30% discount to its net asset value, which has also been greatly reduced.

This is the lowest valuation in a decade for the REIT.

Yes, it's true that the industrial property sector is today oversupplied, and the higher interest rates are also a headwind.

But the situation is not nearly as critical as the market seems to believe.

Even despite these headwinds, the REIT is expected to grow its FFO per share by another 6.7% in 2025. It's not heavily impacted by the surge in rates because the REIT has low leverage with a 25% LTV and well-staggered debt maturities. Moreover, its lease rates are deeply below market, allowing it to significantly bump up rents as its leases gradually expire.

Also, the current oversupply should be only temporary. New construction starts have now dropped to a historically low level, which bodes well for the coming years:

At the same time, the AI revolution has the potential to greatly increase the demand for industrial properties as it could accelerate the growth of e-commerce, all while bringing back more manufacturing to the U.S.

E-commerce benefits from AI as it will allow for more powerful and highly personalized marketing, and it also reduces the cost of shipping thanks to autonomous vehicles. We could also enjoy a boom in domestic manufacturing as the advances in robotics and AI close the cost advantage enjoyed by developing countries.

Rexford is particularly well positioned to profit from this new boom, as it's invested in supply-constrained markets with limited capacity to build more properties.

As its valuation recovers even just halfway to its previous peak, it could unlock 70% upside, and while you wait, you earn a near 5% dividend yield.

Not bad coming from a BBB+ rated blue-chip REIT.

Now, let's look at an even more extreme example.

Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) has seen its share price crash by 67% since 2022, even as its cash flow has grown by 20%.

The market has punished it with a 10-year low valuation, now trading at just 7.5x FFO, and an estimated 40% discount to its NAV.

The life science property sector is today oversupplied, and this has led to stagnating cash flow for Alexandria.

But again, we think that this should be just temporary. New construction activity has dropped to a historic low, even as VC funding is bouncing back.

As the AI revolution takes hold, I expect a new boom in research and development of new drugs. It will accelerate research and lead to the formation of many more new biotech companies as it breaks barriers to entry.

More research also means more experiments, making the labs owned by Alexandria more productive than ever. This will increase the value of the properties to their users and bring back stronger pricing power to their landlords.

Alexandria has commonly traded at a 2-3x higher FFO multiple. Therefore, the upside potential could be 100-200% as this becomes clear to the market. While you wait, you earn a 7.5% dividend yield that's well covered and has been growing steadily:

Closing Note

Every day that passes, more and more people are starting to use AI more seriously, and new AI innovations, including in robotics, are being released at a very fast pace.

I think that this will start to impact corporate profits already in the coming years, and as more investors realize that real estate is the refuge to preserve and grow wealth, capital will start to rotate back to the REIT sector, and this could mark the start of a very rewarding bull market.

REITs are today still trading at fire sale prices, and we have positioned our portfolio at High Yield Landlord to maximize gains through this AI revolution.

data.title: 5 REITs I'm Buying As Housing And Labor Market Downturns Begin
data.description:
Life is full of paradoxes.

And so is the stock market.

One such paradox is the market's resilience -- ebullience, even -- in the face of growing economic headwinds. It seems as though this market is driven primarily by vibes, and the vibes are good. Tariffs have come down to uncomfortable-but-livable levels. President Trump is talking on the phone with President Xi. Overall inflation remains low. Developments in artificial intelligence are exciting and promising. Elon is back to work in the private sector (although now fighting with the president over the "Big Beautiful Bill").

Vibes are good.

Hence, the relatively elevated P/E ratio of over 21x for the S&P 500 (SPY) right now:

And yet... there are some storm clouds gathering.

Sometimes dark clouds gather and then dissipate. Other times, dark clouds gather and escalate into a storm. I'm not a meteorologist and can't tell you what will happen this time, but I'm capable enough to look up into the sky and see the dark clouds rolling in.

Here's where we're going this week:

  • All the ingredients for a mild to moderate downturn in the housing market seem to be in place right now.
  • The cracks forming in the labor market.
  • Why I think many (though by no means all) REITs are attractively valued today.
  • My 5-REIT buy list right now.

Onward.

The Recipe for a Housing Market Downturn

As stated previously, I'm not trying to forecast anything here. Merely to caution.

As the old joke goes, the purpose of economic forecasts is to make meteorologists look good.

At the same time, it does appear as though the makings of a US housing market downturn are forming.

As with all things in economics, the housing market boils down to supply and demand.

Although homebuying demand has been sustained recently by older generations assisting younger generations with down payments, overall demand is depressed due to low affordability.

Affordability is basically determined by the combination of home prices and mortgage rates. Both are elevated right now.

Now, before you jump into the comments to explain to this whippersnapper that your first mortgage in 1981 had an 18% interest rate and that 6-7% mortgage rates are not that high, let me be quick to emphasize that it is the combination of mortgage rates and home prices that impacts affordability.

Home prices in 1981 were far lower than today, even adjusted for inflation.

That's why homebuying affordability is about as low today as it was at the peak of the mid-2000s housing bubble (and before that, the early 1990s), and first-time homebuying affordability (assuming a lower down payment) is around the same level as overall affordability was at the peak of interest rates in the early 1980s.

For homeowners with ample equity built up, higher mortgage rates are much less of a challenge.

The change in mortgage rates has the biggest effect on first-time buyers.

Note, for example, that from the peak of homebuying affordability in the early 2010s to today, the share of first-time homebuyers has halved from 50% to 24%.

And it would be much lower than 24% today if not for down payment assistance from parents and grandparents.

Although the transfer of wealth from older to younger generations has facilitated some first-time homebuying, much of the housing market's resilience over the last few years has been the lingering effects of the surge in homebuying during and immediately after the pandemic.

During this period, buyers massively outnumbered sellers, hence the flood of offers that came in for every listed home and the sustained upward pressure on home prices.

But as you can see above, buyer demand plunged when interest rates soared higher in 2022. Over the course of 2024 and into 2025, the number of sellers in the market has crept steadily higher, forming a big gap between buyers and sellers.

Likewise, homebuilders have kept up a steady pace of construction even amid the depressed buyer demand. Offering credits for customers to buy down mortgage rates has helped to sustain demand for a while, but that trick can only get you so far.

Over the last few years, the number of unsold new homes in homebuilders' inventory has surged to levels not seen since the bursting of the housing bubble.

Some commentators say this is just a normalization of the abnormally tight housing market of the early 2020s, but it doesn't look "normal" to me. Both the absolute level of unsold homes and the trajectory of growth are concerning.

The pendulum swung hard toward housing market tightness during the pandemic, and since then, it seems to be swinging hard in the opposite direction.

Perhaps this is why the stock prices of various homebuilders and materials suppliers (XHB) are down significantly from their highs:

I show Toll Brothers (TOL), a higher-end builder, and KB Home (KBH), a builder of more moderate-priced homes, to show that the whole value spectrum of homebuilders are caught up in the same pullback.

Combining new and existing homes, the unsold for-sale inventory has climbed to a record ~$700 billion.

Although, as you can see from this second chart above, the amount of "stale" inventory that has sat on the market for at least 60 days is not much higher than it was during the second half of the 2010s. Realtors will usually advise their clients to take their homes off the market once they've become stale and try re-listing later.

And yet, even amid this sluggish buyer demand for homes, homebuilders' construction pipeline remains elevated.

Since the beginning of 2020, residential construction spending is up over 50%, outpacing both PPI and construction PPI specifically.

I show the PPI metrics in here to illustrate that residential construction spending is higher even on an inflation-adjusted basis.

The number of homes under construction has fallen from its peak of over 1.7 million in mid-2022 to 1.38 million recently, but that is still higher than pre-pandemic (but post-housing bubble) peak of about 1.2 million homes.

All of the above feels like the recipe for a mild to moderate home price downturn. Some areas are already experiencing such a downturn.

Texas and Florida, two of the hottest states during the boom times several years ago, are now leading this housing market downturn. Where I live in Austin, TX, home prices have already corrected about 12.5%.

Zillow forecasts that nationwide home prices should drop 1.4% this year. If it only drops that much, it will demonstrate the stickiness of home prices and homeowners' collective unwillingness to let home values meaningfully decline.

More pertinently to the economic cycle, though, I would note that housing is arguably the most important cyclical sector of the economy. If this housing market downturn persists or deepens, it could trigger a broader downturn across the economy.

Cracks Forming In The Labor Market

Likewise, the labor market seems to be starting to crack.

While by no means panic-worthy yet, things are looking pretty soft in the labor force.

ADP recently reported that private sector employment grew at a paltry pace (37,000 jobs) in the most recent report. This marks a distinct flattening of employment growth in the private sector compared to the last few years.

That's not a big deal yet, but it could become one.

On top of this slowdown in hiring, we see an uptick in both initial and continuing unemployment claims.

Now, we have had spikes in initial unemployment claims multiple times over the last few years that have not metastasized into anything major. Maybe this will be another of those times. But notice also that there has been a steady upward creep in continuing claims over the last few years, which makes the labor market more fragile now than it was during those previous spikes in initial claims.

Nonfarm payrolls growth has managed to stay fairly strong so far this year, but the consensus economist forecast is for a distinct slowdown in hiring in the second half of the year.

What this portends for the economy, whether a mere slowdown or something more, is above my pay grade.

Why Many REITs Are Attractive Today

Because of my background in real estate, I have a soft spot for REITs. I understand the business model better than any other sector or industry of the market. So that's my bias. I am preferential toward REITs.

Even so, I'd like to briefly make the case for why many (though certainly not all) REITs are very attractively valued for long-term investors today.

The first point to consider is that REITs tend to trade like bond proxies. When bond prices rise (yields fall), REIT stock prices tend to rise as well. When bond prices fall (yields rise), REIT stock prices tend to fall in tandem. REITs are probably the most rate-sensitive sector of the stock market.

This rate-sensitivity was a huge liability during the rising rate environment of 2022-2023. But this year and going forward, the trajectory of interest rates is most likely downward.

Much of the yield curve will be determined by the Fed's moves. Right now, the consensus (illustrated, for example, by the CME's Fedwatch tool) is that the Fed Funds Rate is going down. It's a matter of when, not if.

Currently, the odds of the first rate cut are a little below 50/50 for July and a little above 50/50 for September.

The bottom line is that over the next year or so, the economy is expected to slow down while inflation continues to cool (albeit after an expected rebound due to tariffs), paving the way for the FFR, nominal GDP growth, and the 10-year Treasury rate to all come down.

This should act as a tailwind for REITs.

The second point to consider is that REIT valuations relative to the broader market remain at a depressed level.

This is partly due to REIT stocks' underperformance the last few years, as well as to the S&P 500's strong rally over the same period.

You are simply paying a higher multiple for the average non-real estate stock than you are for the average REIT.

That leads me to my third point because it might be reasonable to discount REITs if their growth prospects were terrible. But they're not.

"FAD" stands for "funds available for distribution," similar to free cash flow.

REIT free cash flows are expected to grow a modest 4% this year, followed by 7.6% next year and an average of 6.6% from 2027 through 2029.

Not bad for a boring bond proxy!

Given the interest rate outlook, valuation discount, and better-than-you'd think growth prospects, REITs look like a good place to hunt for value in today's market.

5 REITs I'm Buying Now

My own REIT buy list is currently made up of 5 high-quality, low-leveraged industry leaders in their respective sub-sectors. Two specialize in senior housing & care facilities, one in mobile home & RV parks, one in Sunbelt grocery-anchored shopping centers, and a final one in Sunbelt apartments.

Dividend Yield Projected Long-Term Dividend Growth Rate (Guesstimate)
American Healthcare REIT (AHR) 2.9% High-Single-Digit to Low-Double-Digit
CareTrust REIT (CTRE) 4.6% High-Single-Digit
Equity LifeStyle Properties (ELS) 3.3% Mid- to High-Single-Digit
InvenTrust Properties (IVT) 3.4% Mid-Single-Digit
Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA) 4.0% Mid-Single-Digit

American Healthcare REIT

I recently wrote a full article on AHR titled "American Healthcare REIT's Growth Story Is Still Young," and I'll point you to that for my full thesis.

In short, AHR owns a high-quality portfolio of senior housing, boasts incredible organic growth potential, enjoys modest leverage, and has a strong cost of equity with which to accretively expand the portfolio. The REIT may not look cheaply valued, but it trades at a significant discount to its close peer in this space, Welltower (WELL).

I wish I'd discovered this REIT sooner, but I can't change the past. We all must live in the now. And right now, AHR strikes me as an attractive buy as a long-term dividend growth investment.

CareTrust REIT

I've also written a fair bit about this triple-net lease senior care REIT, and you can read my most recent full article on it here.

Like AHR, CTRE enjoys a strong cost of equity with which to issue common stock for highly accretive portfolio expansion. Its organic growth is more limited than that of AHR, but the triple-net leased properties CTRE acquires trade at meaningfully higher cap rates to make up for it.

Plus, management has been seriously considering diversifying into the senior housing operating property ("SHOP") space, which would meaningfully increase the REIT's organic growth.

Again, I wish I had bought more at a lower stock price, but CTRE remains a good value for long-term investors even at its current price of around $29 (16.2x FFO).

Equity LifeStyle Properties

Here's part of what I wrote about ELS in last week's article, which was titled "The Simple Path To Dividend Wealth":

ELS is a high-quality REIT that owns manufactured housing communities, RV parks, and marinas across the United States. From its conservative and long-tenured management team to its low-leveraged balance sheet to its consistent cash flows, ELS checks all the boxes of a solid, reliable compounder. It has also raised its dividend for 20 consecutive years.

I couldn't have said it better myself.

Even if ELS enjoys no multiple expansion going forward, I still think it will generate respectable total returns of 8-11% per year, which I can live with.

InvenTrust Properties

Have I written specifically about IVT, you ask? Why, yes, I have. Right around a year ago, I wrote an article titled "Why InvenTrust Is My Top Pick Among Shopping Center REITs."

Since then, there have admittedly been a few other retail REITs that have slightly outperformed IVT. But IVT has outperformed both the SPY and the Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ):

I think IVT is poised to continue that outperformance, based on its modest valuation (15x FFO multiple), low-leveraged balance sheet, solid organic growth prospects from its high-occupancy retail centers in the Sunbelt, and sufficiently low cost of capital to make external acquisitions accretive.

Not to mention the fact that there has been no development boom in the retail sector, which means IVT faces less of the competitive supply pressures that other CRE sectors (like multifamily and industrial) face.

Being a smaller REIT, IVT has a long growth runway ahead of it. I am trying to gradually grow it into a core position in my REIT portfolio.

Mid-America Apartment Communities

Finally, MAA is a blue-chip Sunbelt multifamily REIT with A- and equivalent credit ratings (one of only 10 REITs with an A rating) and a long dividend growth record. Over the past 10-, 15-, and 20-year periods, MAA has generated total returns of around 11% annually but has been rangebound in recent years as its market absorb a 50-year high in new supply.

To quote MAA's CEO Brad Hill from a recent NAREIT presentation:

If you would have told us years ago that we were going to face the highest level of supply we’ve ever seen in our markets, but our NOI was going to go down 1.4%, we wouldn’t have believed that. But that’s what we’ve seen and we’ve performed quite well in the midst of that. So, you know, as we continue to see high levels of supply this year, especially the first half of the year, we continue to see good trends. The market demand remains strong, supply continues to come down from where we are at this point and we do think that we are on the path to continue to deliver strong earnings growth the back half of this year and heading into the next few years where the supply demand dynamics will be materially different than what they are today.

Meanwhile, demand tailwinds continue to be favorable for MAA, not just because homebuying affordability is low but also because population, household, and job growth in MAA's markets is consistently higher than the national average.

Tellingly, for the last three quarters, absorption in MAA's markets has been higher than completions of new supply, indicating that the supply-demand balance has already tilted back in favor of landlords. As long as the economy holds up and homebuying affordability remains low, these favorable conditions should persist.

Market (new lease) rent rates should stabilize over the course of this year and return to growth in 2026 and beyond.

And at some point, management intent to modestly increase leverage from the current net debt to EBITDA of 4x to between 4.5-5x in order to accretively grow the portfolio.

Other than a recession, I don't see what could throw MAA off from its return to mid-single-digit growth starting next year. Home prices and/or mortgage rates would need to come down significantly to bring homebuying costs back into spitting distance with renting costs. That makes MAA's recent stock price dip a good buying opportunity, in my view.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 5 Relatively Secure And Cheap Dividend Stocks, Yields Up To 10% (June 2025)
data.description:
************

Author's Note: This is our monthly series on Dividend Stocks, usually published in the first week of every month. We scan the universe of roughly 7,500 stocks listed and traded on U.S. exchanges and use our proprietary filtering criteria to select five relatively safe stocks that may be trading cheaper compared to their historical valuations. Some of the sections in the article, like "Selection Process/Methodology," are repeated each month with few changes and are presented in "italics." This is intentional as well as unavoidable, as this is necessary for the new readers to be able to conceptualize the process. Regular readers of this series could skip such sections to avoid repetitiveness and jump to the section titled "Selection of the Top 50." Readers can also read a more detailed explanation of the goals and the selection process in our blog post here.

************

Introduction and Market Summary:

What a difference a month or two can make! As we have seen from early April to the end of May, the market sentiment has changed 180 degrees. Back in early April, at the peak of the tariff tantrum, the S&P 500 (SP500) had a brief flirtation with the bear market (below 20% from its recent peak). However, since then, it has recovered almost all of its losses. During this time, the Trump administration lowered the tariff rhetoric and paused almost all of the reciprocal tariffs. In addition, it had a sort of truce with China as well. Also, President Trump's Middle East visit and the promised AI-related investments improved the sentiment for the tech stocks.

That being said, the trade tensions have been heating up a bit once again. Moreover, most trade deals are still elusive and take time to work out. But so far, the market is taking it all in its stride. The market is showing renewed optimism and a level of confidence in a "Trump put." In other words, it has a reasonable level of confidence that President Trump would be sensitive enough to the market and change policies if needed.

Also, on the other developments, the so-called "one big and beautiful bill" that would make the Trump tax cuts of 2017 permanent was passed by Congress and has headed to the Senate. In spite of many positive aspects of the bill for the economy and avoiding a significant tax increase (if the tax cuts of 2017 are not made permanent), the bill is expensive and is expected to increase the debt burden, which is already too high. That is making the bond market somewhat nervous. Any turmoil in the bond market is a risk factor for the stock market going forward.

All that brings back the Fed in focus. So far, the Fed is holding interest rates steady between 4.25% and 4.50% and has not signaled anything more than two cuts in 2025, which will probably be in the second half of the year. Most market participants expect the Fed to stay pat on the rates in its June 18th meeting. However, there is a risk of the Fed being too slow in cutting the rates, just as they were late in raising them in 2022. You can see the current odds of the Fed's next rate cut here on the CME FedWatchTool website.

It is hard to predict where the markets are going to go from here, at least until there is some clarity on some of these issues.

It is always good to have some idea of where the markets and the broader economy may be headed. Even so, we believe markets are always unpredictable, and it is not possible to catch the exact bottom (or the peak). So, it is best to invest regularly and consistently in good, solid dividend-paying stocks when their valuations are reasonable or attractive.

This month, we highlight three groups with five stocks each that have an average dividend yield (as a group) of 4.16%, 5.70%, and 8.13%, respectively. The first list is for conservative and risk-averse investors, while the second is for investors seeking higher yields but still wanting relatively safe dividends. The third group is for yield-hungry investors but comes with an elevated risk, and we urge investors to exercise caution.

The Selection Process:

Goals and Objectives:

The main goal of this series of articles is to shortlist and highlight companies that have a solid history of paying and raising dividends. In addition, we demand that these companies support strong fundamentals, carry low debt, and are offered at a relatively cheaper valuation. These DGI stocks are not going to make anyone rich overnight, but if your goal is to attain financial freedom by owning stocks that would grow dividends over time, meaningfully and sustainably, then you are at the right place. These lists are not necessarily recommendations to buy but a shortlist of probable candidates for further research. Besides, we think each month, if nothing else, this analysis is able to highlight some nuggets that would have otherwise skipped our attention.

Every month, we start with roughly 7,500 stocks that are listed and traded on U.S. exchanges, including over-the-counter (OTC) networks. Using our filtering criteria, the initial list is quickly narrowed to roughly 700 stocks, mostly dividend-paying and dividend-growing. From thereon, by using various data elements, including dividend history, payout ratios, revenue growth, debt ratios, EPS growth, etc., we calculate a "Dividend Quality Score" for each stock that measures the relative safety and sustainability of the dividend. In addition to dividend safety, we also seek cheaper valuations. We also demand that the selected companies have an established business model, solid dividend history, manageable debt, and investment-grade credit rating.

Notes: 1) Please note that when we use the term "safe" in relation to stocks and investments, it should be interpreted as "relatively safe" because nothing is absolutely safe in investing. Even though we present only 5 to 10 stocks in our final list, one should have 15-20 stocks at a minimum in a well-diversified portfolio.

2) All tables in this article are created by the author unless explicitly specified. The stock data have been sourced from various sources such as Seeking Alpha, Yahoo Finance, GuruFocus, Morningstar, and CCC-List (Dividend Radar).

Criteria to Shortlist:

  • Market cap > $8 billion (some exceptions can be made)
  • Dividend yield > 1.0% (some exceptions are made to include high quality but lower yielding companies)
  • Daily average volume > 100,000
  • Dividend growth past five years >= 0.

By applying the above criteria, we got around 600 companies.

Narrowing Down the List

As a first step, we would like to eliminate stocks that have less than five years of dividend growth history. We cross-check our current list of over 600 stocks against the list of so-called Dividend Champions, Contenders, and Challengers originally defined and created by David Fish. Generally, the stocks with more than 25 years of dividend increases are called dividend Champions, while stocks with more than ten but less than 25 years of dividend increases are termed Contenders. Further, stocks with more than five but less than ten years of dividend increases are called Challengers. Also, since we want a lot of flexibility and wider choice at this initial stage, we include some companies that pay dividends lower than the S&P500 but otherwise have a stellar dividend record and growing dividends at a fast pace.

After we apply all the above criteria, we're left with roughly 400 companies on our list. However, so far in this list, we have demanded five or more years of consistent dividend growth. But what if a company had a very stable record of dividend payments but did not increase the dividends from one year to another? At times, some of these companies are foreign-based companies, and due to currency fluctuations, their dividends may appear to have been cut in U.S. dollars, but in reality, that may not be true at all when looked at in the actual currency of reporting. At times, we may provide some exceptions when a company may have cut the dividend in the past, but otherwise, it looks compelling. So, by relaxing some of the conditions, roughly 60-80 additional companies were considered to be on our list. We call them category 'B' companies. After including them, we end up with roughly 450 plus companies that make up our first list.

We then import the various data elements from many sources and assigned weights based on different criteria as listed below:

  • Current yield: Indicates the yield based on the current price.
  • Dividend growth history (number of years of dividend growth): This provides information on how many years a company has paid and increased dividends on a consistent basis. For stocks under the category 'B' (defined above), we consider the total number of consecutive years of dividends paid rather than the number of years of dividend growth.
  • Payout ratio: This indicates how comfortably the company can pay the dividend from its earnings. We prefer this ratio to be as low as possible, which would indicate the company's ability to grow the dividend in the future. This ratio is calculated by dividing the dividend amount per share by the EPS (earnings per share). The cash-flow payout ratio is calculated by dividing the dividend amount paid per share by the cash flow generated per share.
  • Past five-year and 10-year dividend growth: Even though it's the dividend growth rate from the past, this does indicate how fast the company has been able to grow its earnings and dividends in the recent past. The recent past is the best indicator that we have to know what to expect in the next few years.
  • EPS growth (average of previous five years of growth and expected next five years' growth): As the earnings of a company grow, more than likely, dividends will grow accordingly. We will take into account the actual EPS growth of the previous five years and the estimated EPS growth for the next five years. We will add the two numbers and assign weights.
  • Chowder number: So, what's the Chowder number? This number has been named after well-known SA author Chowder, who first coined and popularized this factor. This number is derived by adding the current yield and the past five years' dividend growth rate. A Chowder number of "12" or more ("8" for utilities) is considered good.
  • Debt/equity ratio: This ratio will tell us about the company's debt load in relation to its equity. We all know that too much debt can lead to major problems, even for well-known companies. The lower this ratio, the better it is. Sometimes, we find this ratio to be negative or unavailable, even for well-known companies. This can happen for a myriad of reasons and is not always a reason for concern. This is why we use this ratio in combination with the debt/asset ratio (covered next).
  • Debt/asset ratio: This ratio will tell us about the debt load in relation to the company's total assets. In almost all cases, this ratio would be lower than the debt/equity ratio. Also, this ratio is important because, for some companies, the debt/equity ratio is not a reliable indicator.
  • S&P's credit rating: This is the credit rating assigned by the rating agency S&P Global and is indicative of the company's ability to service its debt. This rating can be obtained from the S&P website.
  • PEG ratio: This is also called the price/earnings-to-growth ratio. The PEG ratio is considered to be an indicator if the stock is overvalued, undervalued, or fairly priced. A lower PEG may indicate that a stock is undervalued. However, PEG for a company may differ significantly from one reported source to another, depending on which growth estimate is used in the calculation. Some use past growth, while others may use future expected growth.
  • Distance from 52-week high: We want to select companies that are good, solid companies but also are trading at cheaper valuations currently. They may be cheaper due to some temporary down cycle or some combination of bad news or simply having a bad quarter. This criterion will help bring such companies (with a cheaper valuation) near the top as long as they excel in other criteria as well. This factor is calculated as (current price - 52-week high) / 52-week high.
  • Sales or Revenue growth: This is the average growth rate in annual sales or revenue of the company over the last five years. A company can only grow its earnings power as long as it can grow its revenue. Sure, it can grow the earnings by cutting costs, but that can't go on forever.

Selection Of The Top 50

Downloadable Dataset: Below, we provide a link to the table with relevant data on nearly 450 stocks. Readers can download this table for further analysis. Please note that the table is sorted by "Total Weight" or "Quality Score."

File-for-export_-_5_Best_DGI_-_June_2025.xlsx

We will first bring down the list (of 400-plus names) to roughly 50 names by automated criteria, as listed below. In the second step, which is mostly manual and subjective, we will bring the list down to about 15.

  • Step 1: First, take the top 20 names from the above table (based on total weight or quality score). At times, some industry segments tend to get overcrowded at the top, so we take the top two (or three) and ignore the rest.
  • Step 2: As a second step, we will take the top 10 names based on the highest dividend yield. When it comes to dividend yield, some of the industry segments tend to be overcrowded. So, we will take the top two (or a maximum of three) names from any single industry segment.
  • Step 3: Now, we will sort our list based on five-year dividend growth (highest at the top) and select the top 10 names.
  • Step 4: Not all companies have a 10-year streak of dividend growth, but many do. We will sort our list based on 10-year dividend growth (highest at the top) and select the top 10 names.
  • Step 5: We also want to give priority to stocks that are rated highest in terms of credit rating. So, we will sort the list based on the numerical weight of the credit rating and select the top 10 stocks with the best credit rating. Again, we are careful not to have too many names from the same sector.
  • Step 6: Lastly, as the name of the series suggests, we want to have some names that may be trading cheaper in comparison to their historical valuation. So, we select the top ten names with the highest discount. However, they could be trading cheap for just the wrong reasons, so we need to be careful that they meet our other quality criteria.

From the above steps, we now have a total of 70 names in our final consideration. However, the following stocks appeared more than once:

Stocks that appeared two times: ARE, CI, DKS, EOG, LYB, MRK, MS, MSFT, PFE, and UNH (10 duplicates).

After removing ten duplicates, we are left with 60 (70-10) names.

Since we still have a very large list, we will remove names that have a low yield and not very great dividend growth. So, if the dividend yield was less than < 2% and the dividend growth (average of 5-year and 10-year) was less than 12%, we would rather remove them at this stage.

So, we remove CBSH and WMT. After removing them, we are left with 58 names.

Since there are still multiple names in each industry segment, we will keep a maximum of two or three names (from the top) from any one segment.

On this basis, we remove a total of 21 names: WLK, NUE, ACN, OLED, MSFT, LRCX, QCOM, ZION, BK, SF, BRO, PH, JNJ, CI, OVV, CTRA, XOM, ET, QSR, TFII, and ATO.

After we remove these 21 names, we are left with 37 names.

The top names from each sector/industry segment are presented:
